title: Summarizing epidemics with AUDPC and AUDPS
text: Area---curve summaries useful goal condense entire disease progress curve single number represents epidemic intensity time. summaries especially helpful scientific question cumulative disease burden rather exact shape epidemic. less informative curves cross, epidemic onset differs strongly among treatments, timing disease increase main response. epifitter currently provides three related functions: AUDPC() area disease progress curve. AUDPC_2_points() estimating AUDPC just two observations logistic assumption. AUDPS() area disease progress stairs, gives balanced weight first last observations. AUDPC() AUDPS(), repeated observations time point now handled explicitly aggregate argument. default, replicated observations averaged per time point area calculated. important practical decision unit summary. Decide whether want one area value mean curve one area value per experimental unit calculating AUDPC AUDPS.

title: Summarizing epidemics with AUDPC and AUDPS
text: start simulated epidemic use disease intensity values across time.
code: set.seed(1)  epi <- sim_logistic(   N = 40,   y0 = 0.01,   dt = 5,   r = 0.25,   alpha = 0.2,   n = 1 )  knitr::kable(epi, digits = 4) ggplot(epi, aes(time, y)) +   geom_point(size = 2, color = "#15616d") +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.9, color = "#15616d") +   labs(     title = "Example disease progress curve",     x = "Time",     y = "Disease intensity"   )

title: Summarizing epidemics with AUDPC and AUDPS
text: AUDPC() uses trapezoidal method summarize curve observed period. absolute value units disease intensity multiplied time. example, severity proportion time days, AUDPC expressed proportion-days. interest scale-free measure, use type = "relative". relative version useful comparing epidemics observed time span different scales.
code: audpc_abs <- AUDPC(   time = epi$time,   y = epi$y,   y_proportion = TRUE,   type = "absolute" )  audpc_abs ## [1] 21.62241 audpc_rel <- AUDPC(   time = epi$time,   y = epi$y,   y_proportion = TRUE,   type = "relative" )  audpc_rel ## [1] 0.5405602

what: Multiple observations at the same time
title: Summarizing epidemics with AUDPC and AUDPS
text: Many experiments include replicated plots assessed dates. setting, area summary computed one value per time point, raw row order. default, AUDPC() AUDPS() aggregate replicated observations using mean time. equivalent calculating area per-time means. robust summary preferred, use median instead mean. want require unique time values catch duplicated assessments error, set aggregate = "none".
code: time_rep <- c(0, 0, 5, 5, 10, 10) y_rep <- c(0.10, 0.30, 0.40, 0.60, 0.70, 0.90)  AUDPC(time = time_rep, y = y_rep) ## [1] 5.75 AUDPS(time = time_rep, y = y_rep) ## [1] 8.25 time_mean <- c(0, 5, 10) y_mean <- c(mean(c(0.10, 0.30)), mean(c(0.40, 0.60)), mean(c(0.70, 0.90)))  AUDPC(time = time_mean, y = y_mean, aggregate = "none") ## [1] 5.75 AUDPS(time = time_mean, y = y_mean, aggregate = "none") ## [1] 8.25 AUDPC(time = time_rep, y = y_rep, aggregate = "median") ## [1] 5.75 AUDPS(time = time_rep, y = y_rep, aggregate = "median") ## [1] 8.25 AUDPC(time = time_rep, y = y_rep, aggregate = "none") AUDPS(time = time_rep, y = y_rep, aggregate = "none")

what: One summary curve versus one value per replicate
title: Summarizing epidemics with AUDPC and AUDPS
text: distinction important experimental data. pass replicated observations together AUDPC() AUDPS(), functions now assume want single summary curve, aggregate repeated observations time point computing area. instead want one AUDPC AUDPS value experimental replicate, compute area separately within replicate. single treatment-level summary can obtained pooling rows using default aggregation time: obtain one value per replicate, group data compute areas within experimental unit. case, use aggregate = "none" replicate contribute one observation per time point. practice: use default aggregation want one area summary mean epidemic across replicates; group replicate want one area summary per experimental unit.
code: epi_rep <- sim_logistic(   N = 30,   y0 = 0.01,   dt = 5,   r = 0.3,   alpha = 0.2,   n = 4 )  knitr::kable(head(epi_rep), digits = 4) AUDPC(time = epi_rep$time, y = epi_rep$random_y) ## [1] 14.70536 AUDPS(time = epi_rep$time, y = epi_rep$random_y) ## [1] 17.20195 epi_rep %>%   group_by(replicates) %>%   summarise(     audpc = AUDPC(time = time, y = random_y, aggregate = "none"),     audps = AUDPS(time = time, y = random_y, aggregate = "none"),     .groups = "drop"   ) %>%   knitr::kable(digits = 4)

title: Summarizing epidemics with AUDPC and AUDPS
text: AUDPS() closely related AUDPC(), uses staircase correction often improves discrimination among curves comparative studies. AUDPC AUDPS emphasize time course differently, report summary used avoid mixing interchangeable.
code: audps_abs <- AUDPS(   time = epi$time,   y = epi$y,   y_proportion = TRUE,   type = "absolute" )  audps_abs ## [1] 24.13622 audps_rel <- AUDPS(   time = epi$time,   y = epi$y,   y_proportion = TRUE,   type = "relative" )  audps_rel ## [1] 0.5363605

title: Summarizing epidemics with AUDPC and AUDPS
text: AUDPC_2_points() helpful initial final disease intensities available logistic epidemic shape assumed. simulated logistic data, estimate close full-curve AUDPC computed intermediate observations.
code: audpc_two_points <- AUDPC_2_points(   time = epi$time[7],   y0 = epi$y[1],   yT = epi$y[7] )  audpc_two_points ## [1] 11.79275 full_curve_audpc <- AUDPC(   time = epi$time,   y = epi$y,   y_proportion = TRUE )  c(   AUDPC_full_curve = full_curve_audpc,   AUDPC_two_points = audpc_two_points ) ## AUDPC_full_curve AUDPC_two_points  ##         21.62241         11.79275

title: Uncertainty bands for fitted disease progress curves
text: Fitted disease progress curves usually shown single line, line estimate. Confidence bands help communicate uncertainty around fitted epidemic curve. article focuses uncertainty fitted mean curve. bands shown plot_fit() prediction intervals future observations, replace full model-based analysis treatment effects. visual tool asking stable fitted curve residual resampling. epifitter, confidence bands added directly plot_fit(): argument conf_int controls whether interval drawn. argument ci_method controls interval estimated. Use intervals compare fitted shapes cautiously. scientific question formal comparison among treatments, use statistical model matches experimental design defining target quantity.
code: plot_fit(fit, conf_int = TRUE, ci_method = "bootstrap", nsim = 500)

title: Uncertainty bands for fitted disease progress curves
text: first simulate one logistic epidemic moderate observational noise fit four linearized epidemic models. noise intentionally visible example confidence bands easy inspect.

title: Uncertainty bands for fitted disease progress curves
text: default bootstrap method resamples residuals fitted curve, creates new plausible disease progress curves observed assessment times, refits model many times, builds band distribution fitted curves. approach assumes residual pattern reasonable basis generating plausible alternative curves. useful visualization, still checked biology system assessment design.  final analyses, increase nsim, example: Larger values nsim make interval stable, also make plot slower compute.
code: plot_fit(   fit,   models = "Logistic",   conf_int = TRUE,   ci_method = "bootstrap",   nsim = 100,   seed = 123 ) plot_fit(fit, conf_int = TRUE, ci_method = "bootstrap", nsim = 500)

title: Uncertainty bands for fitted disease progress curves
text: wild bootstrap method randomly changes sign resampled residuals refitting model. keeps original assessment times intact available ci_method = "wild". Wild bootstrap intervals often useful sensitivity check residual variation changes across range fitted values.

title: Uncertainty bands for fitted disease progress curves

code: plot_fit(   fit,   models = c("Exponential", "Logistic", "Gompertz"),   conf_int = TRUE,   ci_method = "bootstrap",   nsim = 100,   seed = 123 )

title: Uncertainty bands for fitted disease progress curves
text: interface works nonlinear fits returned fit_nlin() fit_nlin2().
code: nlin_fit <- fit_nlin(   time = epi$time,   y = epi$random_y,   starting_par = list(y0 = 0.01, r = 0.1),   maxiter = 200 ) #> Warning: The following nonlinear model(s) did not converge and will be returned #> as NA: Monomolecular.  plot_fit(   nlin_fit,   models = "Logistic",   conf_int = TRUE,   ci_method = "bootstrap",   nsim = 100,   seed = 123 )

title: Uncertainty bands for fitted disease progress curves
text: Use ci_method = "bootstrap" main fitted-curve intervals. Use ci_method = "wild" sensitivity check want second residual bootstrap strategy preserves original assessment times. smooth datasets, confidence band may narrow. Wider bands usually appear observations noisier, fewer assessment times, fitted model less certain. cases, level controls confidence level: bands shown plot_fit() confidence bands fitted curve, prediction intervals future observations. Report nsim, level, fitting method, interval method using plots reports manuscripts. default, plot_fit() keeps fitted curves bands disease proportion scale 0 1. inspect unconstrained fitted values, use:
code: plot_fit(fit, conf_int = TRUE, ci_method = "bootstrap", level = 0.90) plot_fit(fit, conf_int = TRUE, ci_method = "bootstrap", level = 0.95) plot_fit(fit, conf_int = TRUE, y_bounds = NULL)

title: Start here: your first disease progress workflow
text: start simulating logistic epidemic. column y underlying curve random_y represents replicated observations sampling measurement noise.
code: set.seed(1)  epi <- sim_logistic(   N = 80,   y0 = 0.01,   dt = 10,   r = 0.12,   alpha = 0.2,   n = 5 )  knitr::kable(head(epi), digits = 4) ggplot(epi, aes(time, y, group = replicates)) +   geom_point(aes(y = random_y), shape = 1, color = "#8597a4") +   geom_line(color = "#15616d", linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(     title = "Simulated epidemic",     x = "Time",     y = "Disease intensity"   )

title: Start here: your first disease progress workflow

title: Start here: your first disease progress workflow

title: Start here: your first disease progress workflow
text: Many experiments include multiple treatments, cultivars, blocks, locations. Use fit_multi() fitting workflow applied separately group.
code: epi_a <- sim_gompertz(N = 50, y0 = 0.002, dt = 5, r = 0.10, alpha = 0.2, n = 3) epi_b <- sim_gompertz(N = 50, y0 = 0.002, dt = 5, r = 0.14, alpha = 0.2, n = 3)  multi_epi <- bind_rows(epi_a, epi_b, .id = "epidemic")  multi_fit <- fit_multi(   time_col = "time",   intensity_col = "random_y",   data = multi_epi,   strata_cols = "epidemic" )  knitr::kable(head(multi_fit$Parameters), digits = 4)

title: Choosing and fitting disease progress models
text: Disease progress models simplified descriptions epidemic development. useful goal estimate biologically interpretable features initial disease intensity, apparent infection rate, upper asymptote. selected curve looks smooth single fit statistic largest. article explains main model-fitting workflows epifitter: fit_lin() linearized model comparison; fit_nlin() nonlinear fitting two-parameter models; fit_nlin2() nonlinear fitting asymptote K estimated. Use fit_lin() quick screening teaching. Use nonlinear fitting want fit model original disease intensity scale. Use fit_nlin2() epidemic appears plateau 1 estimating maximum disease intensity scientifically meaningful.

title: Choosing and fitting disease progress models
text: Linearized models transform disease intensity fitting straight line. fast often useful model comparison, transformations also change error scale. Always inspect fitted curves original disease intensity scale.
code: set.seed(1) epi <- sim_logistic(N = 60, y0 = 0.01, dt = 5, r = 0.12, alpha = 0.2, n = 4)  fit_lin_out <- fit_lin(time = epi$time, y = epi$random_y) knitr::kable(fit_lin_out$stats_all, digits = 4) plot_fit(fit_lin_out, models = c("Logistic", "Gompertz"))

title: Choosing and fitting disease progress models
text: fit_nlin() fits candidate models directly original disease intensity values. Nonlinear optimization needs starting values, poor starts weakly informative data can lead non-convergence. happens, inspect curve, adjust starting_par, consider whether chosen model identifiable observed time points.
code: fit_nlin_out <- fit_nlin(   time = epi$time,   y = epi$random_y,   starting_par = list(y0 = 0.01, r = 0.03) )  fit_nlin_out$stats_all

title: Choosing and fitting disease progress models
text: default, fit_nlin() fit_nlin2() use ordinary nonlinear least squares. gives every observation weight consistent many traditional disease progress curve workflows. disease incidence severity measured proportion, however, residual variability may change across epidemic. example, binomial-like measurements often variance related p(1−p)p(1 - p), pp fitted disease intensity. epifitter therefore allows optional weighted nonlinear least squares. options working variance assumptions, substitute full binomial beta likelihood. default unchanged: use two-step binomial-like weighting approximation, use weight_method = "binomial". model first fitted without weights, fitted values used construct weights, model refitted using 1/(p(1−p)+ϵ)1 / (p(1 - p) + \epsilon). built-weighting rules available sensitivity analyses: weight_method = "mean" uses 1/(p+ϵ)1 / (p + \epsilon), mean-dependent variance approximation. weight_method = "cv" uses 1/(p2+ϵ)1 / (p^2 + \epsilon), corresponding roughly constant coefficient variation. weight_method = "power" uses 1/(|p|2θ+ϵ)1 / (|p|^{2\theta} + \epsilon), weight_power controls θ\theta. options follow practical idea modeling non-constant variance without leaving nonlinear least-squares framework. universal defaults. Use sensitivity analyses, prefer rule best matches disease intensity measured. can also provide positive weights. useful weights come sample sizes, known measurement precision, variance model chosen outside epifitter. advanced use, weights can also function. function receives data frame time, y, predicted, model, must return one positive weight per observation. grouped nonlinear fitting, use weight_method strategy across curves, weights_col weights stored data. Weighted fits assumptions, automatic improvements. useful residual diagnostics sampling design suggest heteroscedasticity. Compare weighted unweighted fits, inspect residuals, report chosen weighting rule.
code: fit_nlin(   time = epi$time,   y = epi$random_y,   weight_method = "none" ) fit_weighted <- fit_nlin(   time = epi$time,   y = epi$random_y,   weight_method = "binomial",   weight_eps = 0.01 )  fit_weighted$stats_all fit_cv <- fit_nlin(   time = epi$time,   y = epi$random_y,   weight_method = "cv" )  fit_power <- fit_nlin(   time = epi$time,   y = epi$random_y,   weight_method = "power",   weight_power = 0.5 ) custom_weights <- rep(1, nrow(epi))  fit_custom <- fit_nlin(   time = epi$time,   y = epi$random_y,   weights = custom_weights ) fit_custom_fun <- fit_nlin(   time = epi$time,   y = epi$random_y,   weights = function(data) {     1 / (data$predicted + 0.05)   } ) multi_weighted <- fit_multi(   time_col = "time",   intensity_col = "random_y",   data = multi_epi,   strata_cols = "curve",   nlin = TRUE,   weight_method = "binomial" )

what: Estimate K when the epidemic plateaus below 1
title: Choosing and fitting disease progress models
text: two-parameter monomolecular, logistic, Gompertz models assume fixed maximum disease intensity 1. real epidemics, disease may plateau 1 host resistance, limited favorable conditions, management, sampling scale, short observation window. cases, fit_nlin2() estimates upper asymptote K.
code: epi_partial <- epi %>%   mutate(random_y = random_y * 0.8)  fit_k <- fit_nlin2(   time = epi_partial$time,   y = epi_partial$random_y,   starting_par = list(y0 = 0.01, r = 0.03, K = 0.7) )  fit_k$stats_all

title: Choosing and fitting disease progress models
text: Use fit_multi() model-fitting workflow repeated many curves, treatment--block combinations, cultivars, sites, isolates. Keep grouping variables aligned experimental unit.
code: epi1 <- sim_gompertz(N = 50, y0 = 0.001, dt = 5, r = 0.08, alpha = 0.2, n = 3) epi2 <- sim_gompertz(N = 50, y0 = 0.002, dt = 5, r = 0.11, alpha = 0.2, n = 3)  multi_epi <- bind_rows(epi1, epi2, .id = "curve")  multi_fit <- fit_multi(   time_col = "time",   intensity_col = "random_y",   data = multi_epi,   strata_cols = "curve" )  knitr::kable(head(multi_fit$Parameters), digits = 4) knitr::kable(head(multi_fit$Data), digits = 4)

title: Choosing and fitting disease progress models
text: reporting fitted disease progress models, include: disease metric scale, severity proportion incidence proportion; time scale assessment schedule; model family whether K fixed estimated; whether fit used ordinary weighted nonlinear least squares; weighting rule, ; convergence issues models failed fit; uncertainty intervals sensitivity analyses inform interpretation.

title: Real-data workflow: powdery mildew in organic tomato
text: PowderyMildew experimental dataset included epifitter. contains disease progress curves powdery mildew organic tomato different irrigation systems soil moisture levels. data derived study Lage, Marouelli, Cafe-Filho (2019) powdery mildew management different irrigation configurations organic tomato. dataset especially useful demonstrating workflows : real epidemic dataset rather simulated curves; repeated observations time within experimental blocks; treatment comparisons using summary measures fitted models. article intended users want move raw experimental data interpretable epidemic summaries. guiding principle keep experimental unit clear. , block within irrigation--moisture treatment repeatedly assessed time.

title: Real-data workflow: powdery mildew in organic tomato
text: Use usual data() interface packaged datasets. dataset contains five variables: irrigation_type irrigation system; moisture soil moisture regime; block experimental block; time assessment date; sev disease severity proportional scale.
code: data("PowderyMildew") knitr::kable(head(PowderyMildew, 12), digits = 4)

title: Real-data workflow: powdery mildew in organic tomato
text: repeated-measures dataset: block assessed across several time points within irrigation--moisture combination. treatment comparisons, block-level curve unit produce one summary value one fitted model. Pooling blocks together useful visualization treatment means, changes inferential target.
code: PowderyMildew %>%   distinct(irrigation_type, moisture, block) %>%   arrange(irrigation_type, moisture, block) %>%   knitr::kable()

title: Real-data workflow: powdery mildew in organic tomato
text: useful first step summarize replicated blocks within treatment visualize mean disease progress curve.
code: pm_summary <- PowderyMildew %>%   group_by(irrigation_type, moisture, time) %>%   summarise(     mean_sev = mean(sev),     .groups = "drop"   )  knitr::kable(head(pm_summary, 12), digits = 4) ggplot(pm_summary, aes(time, mean_sev, color = irrigation_type)) +   geom_point(size = 1.8) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +   facet_wrap(~ moisture) +   labs(     title = "Mean powdery mildew progress by treatment",     x = "Time",     y = "Mean severity",     color = "Irrigation"   )

what: Calculate AUDPC and AUDPS per experimental unit
title: Real-data workflow: powdery mildew in organic tomato
text: block identifies repeated experimental units, common analysis calculate one summary value per block within treatment. differs pooling rows together. blocks passed , AUDPC() AUDPS() assume want one treatment-level summary curve aggregate repeated observations time point calculating area. Statistically, block-level values can later analyzed methods match experimental design, models including treatment terms block structure. epifitter computes epidemiological summaries; downstream treatment analysis still respect randomization, blocking, replication. compare treatments visually, often useful plot one point per block top boxplot.
code: pm_area <- PowderyMildew %>%   group_by(irrigation_type, moisture, block) %>%   summarise(     audpc = AUDPC(time = time, y = sev, aggregate = "none"),     audps = AUDPS(time = time, y = sev, aggregate = "none"),     .groups = "drop"   )  knitr::kable(pm_area, digits = 4) pm_area_long <- pm_area %>%   tidyr::pivot_longer(     cols = c(audpc, audps),     names_to = "summary_measure",     values_to = "value"   )  facet_aud <- if (requireNamespace("lemon", quietly = TRUE)) {   lemon::facet_rep_grid(summary_measure ~ moisture, scales = "free_y") } else {   ggplot2::facet_grid(summary_measure ~ moisture, scales = "free_y") }  ggplot(   pm_area_long,   aes(irrigation_type, value, fill = irrigation_type) ) +   geom_boxplot(     width = 0.65,     outlier.shape = NA,     alpha = 0.28,     linewidth = 0.6   ) +   geom_jitter(     aes(color = irrigation_type),     width = 0.12,     height = 0,     size = 2.4,     alpha = 0.85   ) +   facet_aud +   labs(     title = "Area-under-the-curve summaries by treatment",     x = "Irrigation treatment",     y = "Summary value",     color = "Irrigation"   ) +   cowplot::theme_half_open(font_size = 12) +   background_grid(major = "y", minor = "none") +   theme(     legend.position = "none",     axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1),     strip.background = element_rect(fill = "#e7f1f3", color = NA),     strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")   )

what: Fit a model to one observed curve
title: Real-data workflow: powdery mildew in organic tomato
text: work single disease progress curve, first filter one treatment--block combination.  Now fit candidate models curve. fitted parameters descriptive summaries selected curve. example, apparent infection rate meaningful relation model form, disease scale, observed time interval.
code: single_curve <- PowderyMildew %>%   filter(     irrigation_type == "Drip",     moisture == "High moisture",     block == 1   )  knitr::kable(single_curve, digits = 4) ggplot(single_curve, aes(time, sev)) +   geom_point(size = 2, color = "#15616d") +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.9, color = "#15616d") +   labs(     title = "Single observed disease progress curve",     subtitle = "Drip irrigation, high moisture, block 1",     x = "Time",     y = "Severity"   ) single_fit <- fit_lin(time = single_curve$time, y = single_curve$sev) knitr::kable(single_fit$stats_all, digits = 4) plot_fit(single_fit, point_size = 2, line_size = 0.9)

what: Fit models to many observed curves at once
title: Real-data workflow: powdery mildew in organic tomato
text: fit_multi() useful treatment--block combination fitted separately. output keeps original stratification variables, makes easier compare fitted parameters among irrigation systems, moisture regimes, blocks. formal treatment comparisons fitted parameters, treat outputs derived variables analyze using model consistent trial design. Avoid treating repeated assessments block independent treatment replicates.
code: pm_multi_fit <- fit_multi(   time_col = "time",   intensity_col = "sev",   data = PowderyMildew,   strata_cols = c("irrigation_type", "moisture", "block") )  knitr::kable(head(pm_multi_fit$Parameters, 12), digits = 4)

text: PowderyMildew dataset included epifitter comes : Lage, D. . C., Marouelli, W. ., Cafe-Filho, . C. (2019). Management powdery mildew behaviour late blight different irrigation configurations organic tomato. Crop Protection, 125, 104886.

title: Simulation workflows for disease progress curves
text: sim_ family creates synthetic disease progress curves match model families used fitting functions. Simulations useful teaching, testing analysis workflows, checking whether model can recover known parameters, generating reproducible examples. substitute biological validation. Choose parameter values make sense host-pathogen system, disease metric, time scale.

title: Simulation workflows for disease progress curves
text: four model families represent different epidemic shapes. Exponential growth appropriate early unconstrained increase, monomolecular, logistic, Gompertz curves include different forms deceleration disease approaches upper limit.
code: exp_model <- sim_exponential(N = 100, y0 = 0.01, dt = 10, r = 0.045, alpha = 0.2, n = 5) mono_model <- sim_monomolecular(N = 100, y0 = 0.01, dt = 5, r = 0.05, alpha = 0.2, n = 5) log_model <- sim_logistic(N = 100, y0 = 0.01, dt = 5, r = 0.10, alpha = 0.2, n = 5) gomp_model <- sim_gompertz(N = 100, y0 = 0.01, dt = 5, r = 0.07, alpha = 0.2, n = 5) exp_plot <- ggplot(exp_model, aes(time, y)) +   geom_jitter(aes(y = random_y), width = 0.1, color = "#6c757d") +   geom_line(color = "#b56576", linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(title = "Exponential")  mono_plot <- ggplot(mono_model, aes(time, y)) +   geom_jitter(aes(y = random_y), width = 0.1, color = "#6c757d") +   geom_line(color = "#588157", linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(title = "Monomolecular")  log_plot <- ggplot(log_model, aes(time, y)) +   geom_jitter(aes(y = random_y), width = 0.1, color = "#6c757d") +   geom_line(color = "#355070", linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(title = "Logistic")  gomp_plot <- ggplot(gomp_model, aes(time, y)) +   geom_jitter(aes(y = random_y), width = 0.1, color = "#6c757d") +   geom_line(color = "#8d5a97", linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(title = "Gompertz") plot_grid(exp_plot, mono_plot, log_plot, gomp_plot, ncol = 2)

what: Send simulated data into the fitting pipeline
title: Simulation workflows for disease progress curves
text: using simulations method checking, compare fitted model known data-generating model. using simulations teaching, show underlying curve (y) noisy observations (random_y) users can distinguish epidemic process observation process.
code: fit_from_sim <- fit_lin(time = log_model$time, y = log_model$random_y) fit_from_sim$stats_all ## # A tibble: 4 × 14 ##   best_model model      r    r_se r_ci_lwr r_ci_upr    v0  v0_se r_squared   RSE ##        <int> <chr>  <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl> ## 1          1 Logi… 0.101  6.96e-4   0.0998   0.103  -4.65 0.0407     0.995 0.216 ## 2          2 Gomp… 0.0723 1.50e-3   0.0694   0.0753 -2.41 0.0879     0.957 0.467 ## 3          3 Mono… 0.0559 2.02e-3   0.0519   0.0599 -1.10 0.118      0.881 0.628 ## 4          4 Expo… 0.0453 1.99e-3   0.0413   0.0492 -3.55 0.116      0.834 0.617 ## # ℹ 4 more variables: CCC <dbl>, y0 <dbl>, y0_ci_lwr <dbl>, y0_ci_upr <dbl>

what: An R package for analysis and simulation of plant disease progress curves
title: An R package for analysis and simulation of plant disease progress curves
text: epifitter provides tools visualization, description, comparison plant disease progress curves (DPCs). DPC describes disease intensity changes time epidemic. fitting classic population dynamics models logistic, monomolecular, Gompertz, exponential curves, users can compare epidemics better understand epidemiological behavior. epifitter wraps workflows package-oriented interface includes model fitting, summary measures, simulation helpers, plotting functions tailored plant disease epidemiology. Current functionality includes: Fit classic population dynamics models using linear nonlinear approaches Fit nonlinear models optional built-custom weighting proportional disease intensity data Select models based statistical visual analysis Calculate area disease progress curve Compare epidemics via visual inference Simulate synthetic epidemics various shapes uncertainty

title: An R package for analysis and simulation of plant disease progress curves
text: Compare epidemic multiple canonical disease progress models. Move simulation fitting consistent data structure. Work single epidemics grouped data using package vocabulary. Produce ggplot2-ready outputs reports, papers, teaching material.

title: An R package for analysis and simulation of plant disease progress curves
text: Install stable release CRAN: Install development version GitHub pak:
code: install.packages("epifitter") if (!requireNamespace("pak", quietly = TRUE)) {   install.packages("pak") }  pak::pak("AlvesKS/epifitter")

title: An R package for analysis and simulation of plant disease progress curves
text: complete workflows, see package articles model fitting, area summaries, simulation, bundled experimental dataset documentation site.
code: library(epifitter)  set.seed(1) epi <- sim_logistic(N = 30, y0 = 0.01, dt = 5, r = 0.3, alpha = 0.2, n = 4) fit <- fit_lin(time = epi$time, y = epi$y)  plot_fit(fit)

what: Area under the disease progress curve — AUDPC
title: Area under the disease progress curve — AUDPC
text: Calculate area disease progress curve using trapezoidal method.

title: Area under the disease progress curve — AUDPC
code: AUDPC(   time,   y,   y_proportion = TRUE,   type = "absolute",   aggregate = c("mean", "median", "none") )

title: Area under the disease progress curve — AUDPC
text: time numeric vector assessment times. y numeric vector disease intensity values. y_proportion Logical. `y` values expressed proportions? type Either `"absolute"` `"relative"`. aggregate handle multiple observations time point. default, `"mean"`, averages replicated observations calculating area. `"median"` uses median `"none"` requires unique time values.

title: Area under the disease progress curve — AUDPC
text: numeric scalar AUDPC value.

title: Area under the disease progress curve — AUDPC
text: Madden, L. V., Hughes, G., van den Bosch, F. (2007). Study Plant Disease Epidemics. American Phytopathological Society.

title: Area under the disease progress curve — AUDPC
code: epi <- sim_logistic(N = 30, y0 = 0.01, dt = 5, r = 0.3, alpha = 0.5, n = 1) AUDPC(time = epi$time, y = epi$y, y_proportion = TRUE) #> [1] 14.79107

what: Fit models to multiple disease progress curves — fit_multi
title: Fit models to multiple disease progress curves — fit_multi
text: Apply `fit_lin()`, `fit_nlin()`, `fit_nlin2()` multiple disease progress curves stored data frame.

title: Fit models to multiple disease progress curves — fit_multi
code: fit_multi(   time_col,   intensity_col,   data,   strata_cols = NULL,   starting_par = list(y0 = 0.01, r = 0.03, K = 0.8),   maxiter = 500,   nlin = FALSE,   estimate_K = FALSE,   weights_col = NULL,   weight_method = c("none", "binomial", "mean", "cv", "power"),   weight_eps = 0.01,   weight_power = 1 )

title: Fit models to multiple disease progress curves — fit_multi
text: time_col Character name specifying time column. intensity_col Character name specifying disease intensity column. data data frame containing variables model fitting. strata_cols Character vector specifying grouping columns. Use `NULL` fit rows single epidemic. Defaults `NULL`. starting_par Named list starting values model parameters. maxiter Maximum number iterations nonlinear fitting. Must positive number. nlin Logical. nonlinear fitting used? estimate_K Logical. asymptote `K` estimated? weights_col Optional character name specifying column positive weights weighted nonlinear least squares. Used `nlin = TRUE`. weight_method Weighting strategy passed [fit_nlin()] [fit_nlin2()]. Use `"none"` ordinary nonlinear least squares `"binomial"`, `"mean"`, `"cv"`, `"power"` two-step fitted-value weighting approximations. weight_eps Small positive constant used fitted-value weighting methods keep weights finite near 0 1. weight_power Non-negative power used `weight_method = "power"`.

title: Fit models to multiple disease progress curves — fit_multi
text: list grouped parameter estimates prediction data.

title: Fit models to multiple disease progress curves — fit_multi

what: Fit epidemic models using nonlinear regression — fit_nlin
title: Fit epidemic models using nonlinear regression — fit_nlin
text: Fit exponential, monomolecular, logistic, Gompertz models disease progress data using nonlinear regression.

title: Fit epidemic models using nonlinear regression — fit_nlin
code: fit_nlin(   time,   y,   starting_par = list(y0 = 0.01, r = 0.03),   maxiter = 50,   weights = NULL,   weight_method = c("none", "binomial", "mean", "cv", "power"),   weight_eps = 0.01,   weight_power = 1 )

title: Fit epidemic models using nonlinear regression — fit_nlin
text: time Numeric vector assessment times. y Numeric vector disease intensity values. starting_par Named list starting values `y0` `r`. omitted partially specified, `epifitter` supplies data-driven fallback values. maxiter Maximum number iterations. Must positive number. weights Optional numeric vector positive weights, function receives data frame columns `time`, `y`, `predicted`, `model` returns positive weights. supplied, weights used directly `weight_method` must `"none"`. weight_method Weighting strategy nonlinear least squares. Use `"none"` ordinary nonlinear least squares, `"binomial"` \(1 / (\hat{p}(1 - \hat{p}) + \epsilon)\), `"mean"` \(1 / (\hat{p} + \epsilon)\), `"cv"` \(1 / (\hat{p}^2 + \epsilon)\), `"power"` \(1 / (|\hat{p}|^{2\theta} + \epsilon)\). weight_eps Small positive constant added fitted-value variance approximations keep weights finite near 0 1. weight_power Non-negative power \(\theta\) used `weight_method = "power"`.

title: Fit epidemic models using nonlinear regression — fit_nlin
text: list fit statistics, parameter estimates, prediction   data.

title: Fit epidemic models using nonlinear regression — fit_nlin
text: Weighted fits use weighted nonlinear least squares, binomial beta likelihood. `weight_method` options `"none"` use two-step working approximation: first fit model without weights, derive weights fitted values, refit model. Report selected weighting rule assumption.

title: Fit epidemic models using nonlinear regression — fit_nlin
code: set.seed(1) epi <- sim_logistic(N = 30, y0 = 0.01, dt = 5, r = 0.3, alpha = 0.2, n = 4) fit_nlin(time = epi$time, y = epi$random_y, starting_par = list(y0 = 0.01, r = 0.03)) #> Warning: The following nonlinear model(s) did not converge and will be returned as NA: Monomolecular. #> Results of fitting population models  #>  #> Stats: #>                  CCC r_squared    RSE #> Gompertz      0.9986    0.9981 0.0217 #> Logistic      0.9986    0.9974 0.0220 #> Exponential   0.9038    0.8493 0.1665 #> Monomolecular     NA        NA     NA #>  #>  Infection rate: #>                 Estimate   Std.error      Lower     Upper #> Gompertz      0.22500335 0.006307726 0.21203764 0.2379691 #> Logistic      0.31571569 0.009477990 0.29623341 0.3351980 #> Exponential   0.06632831 0.007801830 0.05029142 0.0823652 #> Monomolecular         NA          NA         NA        NA #>  #>  Initial inoculum: #>                   Estimate    Std.error         Lower        Upper #> Gompertz      1.279015e-08 2.122676e-08 -3.084208e-08 5.642237e-08 #> Logistic      8.926801e-03 1.286286e-03  6.282802e-03 1.157080e-02 #> Exponential   1.560581e-01 3.149510e-02  9.131899e-02 2.207972e-01 #> Monomolecular           NA           NA            NA           NA

what: Fit epidemic models and estimate the asymptote — fit_nlin2
title: Fit epidemic models and estimate the asymptote — fit_nlin2
text: Fit monomolecular, logistic, Gompertz epidemic models using nonlinear regression also estimating maximum disease intensity parameter `K`.

title: Fit epidemic models and estimate the asymptote — fit_nlin2
code: fit_nlin2(   time,   y,   starting_par = list(y0 = 0.01, r = 0.03, K = 0.8),   maxiter = 50,   weights = NULL,   weight_method = c("none", "binomial", "mean", "cv", "power"),   weight_eps = 0.01,   weight_power = 1 )

title: Fit epidemic models and estimate the asymptote — fit_nlin2
text: time Numeric vector assessment times. y Numeric vector disease intensity values. starting_par Named list starting values `y0`, `r`, `K`. omitted partially specified, `epifitter` supplies data-driven fallback values. maxiter Maximum number iterations. Must positive number. weights Optional numeric vector positive weights, function receives data frame columns `time`, `y`, `predicted`, `model` returns positive weights. supplied, weights used directly `weight_method` must `"none"`. weight_method Weighting strategy nonlinear least squares. Use `"none"` ordinary nonlinear least squares, `"binomial"` \(1 / (\hat{p}(1 - \hat{p}) + \epsilon)\), `"mean"` \(1 / (\hat{p} + \epsilon)\), `"cv"` \(1 / (\hat{p}^2 + \epsilon)\), `"power"` \(1 / (|\hat{p}|^{2\theta} + \epsilon)\). weight_eps Small positive constant added fitted-value variance approximations keep weights finite near 0 1. weight_power Non-negative power \(\theta\) used `weight_method = "power"`.

title: Fit epidemic models and estimate the asymptote — fit_nlin2
text: list fit statistics, parameter estimates, prediction   data.

title: Fit epidemic models and estimate the asymptote — fit_nlin2
text: Weighted fits use weighted nonlinear least squares, binomial beta likelihood. `weight_method` options `"none"` use two-step working approximation: first fit model without weights, derive weights fitted values, refit model. Report selected weighting rule assumption.

title: Fit epidemic models and estimate the asymptote — fit_nlin2
code: set.seed(1) epi <- sim_logistic(N = 30, y0 = 0.01, dt = 5, r = 0.3, alpha = 0.5, n = 4) fit_nlin2(   time = epi$time,   y = epi$random_y * 0.8,   starting_par = list(y0 = 0.01, r = 0.1, K = 0.8),   maxiter = 1024 ) #> Warning: no non-missing arguments to max; returning -Inf #> Warning: no non-missing arguments to max; returning -Inf #> Warning: no non-missing arguments to max; returning -Inf #> Warning: The following nonlinear model(s) with estimated K did not converge and will be returned as NA: Monomolecular. #> Results of fitting population models  #>  #> Stats: #>                  CCC r_squared    RSE #> Gompertz      0.9954    0.9915 0.0333 #> Logistic      0.9646    0.9370 0.0907 #> Monomolecular     NA        NA     NA #>  #>  Infection rate: #>                Estimate  Std.error     Lower     Upper #> Gompertz      0.2870933 0.02151228 0.2427880 0.3313987 #> Logistic      0.1820370 0.03123483 0.1177076 0.2463663 #> Monomolecular        NA         NA        NA        NA #>  #>  Initial inoculum: #>                   Estimate    Std.error         Lower        Upper #> Gompertz      2.220446e-16 2.149695e-15 -4.205336e-15 4.649425e-15 #> Logistic      3.923556e-02 1.668975e-02  4.862385e-03 7.360874e-02 #> Monomolecular           NA           NA            NA           NA #>  #>  Maximum disease intensity: #>                Estimate  Std.error     Lower     Upper #> Gompertz      0.7766917 0.01357448 0.7487345 0.8046489 #> Logistic      1.0000000 0.09237762 0.8097447 1.1902553 #> Monomolecular        NA         NA        NA        NA

title: Plot fitted epidemic models — plot_fit
text: Create faceted `ggplot2` panel showing observed fitted values selected epidemic models. Optionally, add confidence bands around fitted curves.

title: Plot fitted epidemic models — plot_fit
code: plot_fit(   object,   point_size = 1.2,   line_size = 1,   models = c("Exponential", "Monomolecular", "Logistic", "Gompertz"),   conf_int = FALSE,   ci_method = c("bootstrap", "wild"),   nsim = 500,   level = 0.95,   seed = NULL,   n_grid = 100,   ci_alpha = 0.2,   y_bounds = c(0, 1) )

title: Plot fitted epidemic models — plot_fit
text: object fitted object returned `fit_lin()`, `fit_nlin()`, `fit_nlin2()`. point_size Point size observed values. line_size Line width fitted curves. models Character vector models display. conf_int Logical. `TRUE`, draw confidence bands around fitted curves. ci_method Method used estimate confidence bands. Use `"bootstrap"` residual bootstrap intervals, `"wild"` wild residual bootstrap intervals. older `"case"` option accepted deprecated alias `"wild"`. nsim Number bootstrap samples used `conf_int = TRUE`. level Confidence level interval. seed Optional random seed used interval estimation. n_grid Number time points used draw fitted curves confidence bands. ci_alpha Transparency confidence band. y_bounds Numeric vector length two used constrain plotted fitted values confidence bands. default keeps disease intensity usual proportion scale 0 1. Use `NULL` show unconstrained fitted values.

title: Plot fitted epidemic models — plot_fit
text: `ggplot2` object.

title: Plot fitted epidemic models — plot_fit
code: epi <- sim_logistic(N = 30, y0 = 0.01, dt = 5, r = 0.3, alpha = 0.2, n = 4) fit <- fit_lin(time = epi$time, y = epi$random_y) plot_fit(fit)  # \donttest{ plot_fit(fit, conf_int = TRUE, nsim = 100)  # }

text: Simulate disease progress data monomolecular epidemic model, optional replicated observations.

title: Simulate a monomolecular disease progress curve — sim_monomolecular
code: sim_monomolecular(N = 10, dt = 1, y0 = 0.01, r, K = 1, n, alpha = 0.2)

title: Simulate a monomolecular disease progress curve — sim_monomolecular
text: N Total epidemic duration. Must positive. dt Time interval assessments. Must positive less equal `N`. y0 Initial disease intensity proportion, strictly 0 1. r Apparent infection rate. Must positive. K Maximum disease intensity proportion. Must greater equal `y0` less equal 1. n Number replicated curves. Must positive whole number. alpha Non-negative noise level applied replicated observations.

epifitter 1.0.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-04-12

title: epifitter 1.0.0
text: Modernized fitting workflow shared input validation, informative error messages, improved grouped-model ergonomics. Refreshed documentation site updated articles, improved accessibility, dark mode support, clearer visual style figures. Added formal package citation, vignette built around bundled PowderyMildew dataset, expanded regression tests area summaries model fitting. Corrected area---curve handling repeated assessment times strengthened defaults nonlinear fitting workflows. Added optional confidence bands plot_fit() residual wild bootstrap interval methods. Strengthened simulation input validation corrected logistic simulation equation K lower 1. Added optional weighted nonlinear least squares fit_nlin() fit_nlin2(), including custom numeric weights, custom weight functions, fitted-value weighting methods ("binomial", "mean", "cv", "power") proportional disease intensity data.
